attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with light vaginal spotting. She is eight weeks pregnant by her last menstrual period. This is her first pregnancy and she has no significant medical history. She does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her abdomen is soft and non-tender. Speculum examination reveals a closed cervical os with minimal dark blood in the vaginal vault. A transvaginal ultrasound reveals an intrauterine gestational sac with no visible yolk sac or embryo. The mean sac diameter is measured at 28 mm.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Discharge with follow-up in antenatal clinic
Serial serum beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a confirmed early pregnancy loss based on transvaginal ultrasound findings. A mean gestational sac diameter greater than 25 mm without a visible yolk sac is diagnostic of early pregnancy loss and no further investigations are required.

Offering management options for miscarriage is appropriate as this is a confirmed loss. The patient should be counselled about three options: expectant management (allowing natural passage of products), medical management (using misoprostol), or surgical management (suction curettage). The choice depends on patient preference, clinical circumstances, and local resources.

Repeat ultrasound in one week is unnecessary as the current findings are diagnostic of pregnancy loss. Further scanning would only delay definitive management and potentially cause additional emotional distress.

Discharge with follow-up in antenatal clinic would be inappropriate as this is not a viable pregnancy. The patient requires counselling about miscarriage management rather than routine antenatal care.

Serial serum beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements are not indicated when ultrasound findings are diagnostic of pregnancy loss. These measurements are more useful in cases where ultrasound findings are inconclusive or when monitoring for complete passage of products.

Expectant management only is too restrictive. While expectant management is a valid option, the patient should be offered all available management options to make an informed choice. Some women may prefer medical or surgical management for various reasons including predictability and time to completion.
learningPoints: A mean gestational sac diameter >25 mm without a yolk sac is diagnostic of early pregnancy loss.
Women with confirmed early pregnancy loss should be offered options for management including expectant, medical, or surgical approaches.
Transvaginal ultrasound is the gold standard for diagnosing early pregnancy loss.
references: Queensland Health. Guideline for the early pregnancy loss: diagnosis and management [Internet]. Brisbane (QLD): Queensland Health; [cited 2024 Dec 6]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0033/139947/g-epl.pdf

Mercy Perinatal. Ultrasound criteria for miscarriage [Internet]. Heidelberg (VIC): Mercy Perinatal; [cited 2024 Dec 6]. Available from: https://mercyperinatal.com/images/uploads/resources/Ultrasound-criteria-for-miscarriage.pdf
